For genericchargeassignments,anomalycancellationrequiresnewfermions(anomalons) that are chiral under the new abelian symmetry and carry electroweak charges. If their masses arise from the breaking of the new gauge symmetry, integrating them out generates WessвҖ“Zumino interactions fixed by mixed-anomaly matching, provid- ingtheinfrareddescriptionofthetheory. Motivated by recent NA62 and Belle II results, we then develop a unified phenomenological framework for the missing-energy signatures of these anomalous light vectors, focus- ing on scenarios where the new vector decays predominantly into neutrinos so that the leading probes are rare processes with invisible final states. Within this landscape, light spin-1 bosons are particularly well motivated. The standard benchmark is a secluded U(1) gauge boson kinetically mixed with the photon [1], yielding universal couplings to the Standard Model (SM) electromagnetic current. While predictive, this setup can be too restrictive, and more general interactions of a light vector with SM fields are also worth considering. A theoretically motivated route beyond kinetic mixing is obtained by gauging the accidental global symmetries of the SM in the limit of massless neutrinos, namely baryon number U(1) and family lepton numbers U(1) B L i (i = e,Ој,П„). Within the SM field content, only the combinations L вҲ’L are anomaly free [2вҖ“4]. i j Hence, to gauge a general linear combination (cid:88) X = Оұ B + Оұ L , (1.1) B i i i=e,Ој,П„ one must introduce new fermions (вҖңanomalonsвҖқ) to cancel the U(1) anomalies, including X mixed anomalies with SU(2) Г—U(1) . In the regime of interest for a light vector, g вүӘ 1, a light X can X Ој coexist with a large v , thereby pushing the ultraviolet (UV) completion (the anomalons) to X m вү« m unless the Yukawas are exceedingly small. A central question is then how heavy this ОЁ X UV sector can be. Here we adopt the guiding perspective of вҖңfinite naturalnessвҖқ [6], and apply naturalness criteria to finite contributions to the Higgs mass. Requiring that the anomalons do not induce an excessively large finite correction to the Higgs mass yields an upper bound on m , typically pointing to a multi-TeV scale for percent-level tuning. This provides a concrete ОЁ target for the UV completion of light vectors coupled to anomalous SM currents. If the anomalons are heavier than the EW scale, their impact on the low-energy physics of X is captured by an effective field theory (EFT). Integrating out the anomalons gener- Ој ates dimension-4 WessвҖ“Zumino (WZ) terms of the schematic form X(WвҲӮW + WWW) and XBвҲӮB, which reproduce the anomalous variation of the effective action associated with the anomalous SM current coupled to X (see e.g. [7вҖ“10]). In the limit where anomalon masses Ој originate from a SM-preserving VEV, the WZ coefficients are fully fixed by the requirement of canceling the [SU(2)2U(1) ] and [U(1)2 U(1) ] anomalies of the SM sector [11, 12]. Since WZ L X Y X interactions exhibit an axion-like behaviour (equivalence theorem for the longitudinal mode of X ), they induce amplitudes growing with energy [11, 13], leading to characteristic collider and Ој flavor signatures. In particular, the XWвҲӮW operator can source loop-induced flavor-violating processes, following a predictive flavor pattern which resembles the minimal flavor violation hypothesis [14], while XBвҲӮB contributes to Z вҶ’ ОіX at tree level (see e.g. [15вҖ“18]). 2 In this work we use this IR description to develop a general phenomenological framework for missing-energy searches for light vectors.

Motivated in particular by the recent NA62 [19вҖ“ 21] and Belle II [22] results on rare meson decays with missing energy, we focus on scenarios where the dominant SM decay mode of the vector X is into neutrinos, so that the leading probes are rare processes with invisible final states. These include radiative Z decays and rare K, D, and B transitions with missing energy, which together provide complementary sensitivity to the WZ interactions and to the underlying mixed-anomaly structure. We then specializetovector gaugecurrentsoftheforminEq.(1.1)andtotheirminimalUVcompletions, complementing previous work where we analyzed light vectors coupled to chiral currents [23]. We classify the minimal anomalon spectra required by mixed-anomaly cancellation, derive the corresponding WZ coefficients in the EFT, and combine finite naturalness considerations with phenomenological constraints to delineate the viable parameter space. A representative example within this broader framework is provided by models based on a gauged П„-flavor symmetry: if the decay X вҶ’ П„+П„вҲ’ is kinematically forbidden, the vector can dominantly decay invisibly into neutrinos, yielding signals such as B вҶ’ K(вҲ—)X [23]. Such a setup could account for the 2.7Пғ excess reported by Belle II in B+ вҶ’ K+E [22]. Ref. ОЁ 3 Missing-energy signatures of light vectors In this section we develop a general phenomenological framework for missing-energy searches of light vectors coupled to electroweak-anomalous currents.

In the mass range of interest, the dominant SM decay mode of these vectors can be into neutrinos, so that the leading probes are rare processes with invisible final states. In particular, the WZ interactions generated by integrating out the electroweak anomalons induce characteristic signals in radiative Z decays and in rare meson transitions with missing energy, providing complementary sensitivity across the K, D, and B sectors. We begin with an EFT description of the relevant low-energy couplings in terms of WZ operators, keeping the discussion as model independent as possible. We then review the main experimental searches for E signatures in Z, K, D, and B decays, and summarize the miss corresponding constraints and projections. As a representative example within this broader framework, we discuss the gauged П„-flavor symmetry benchmark (corresponding to Оұ = Оұ = B e Оұ = 0 and Оұ = 1 in Eq. (1.1)), motivated by the fact that a П„-philic vector can dominantly Ој П„ decay invisibly into neutrinos when X вҶ’ П„+П„вҲ’ is kinematically forbidden, yielding signals such as B вҶ’ K(вҲ—)X [23]. This setup has been considered as a possible explanation of the 2.7Пғ excess reported by Belle II in B+ вҶ’ K+E [22], and explicit UV completions were constructed miss in Ref. [23] for light vectors coupled to chiral currents. Here we instead focus on the more minimal case of vector gauge currents in Eq. (1.1) and use the П„-flavor benchmark to illustrate the interplay between missing-energy searches and the IR WZ description. The connection to explicit UV completions and to direct searches for anomalons is discussed in Section 4, where we build representative models based on the anomaly-canceling spectra identified in Section 2. 3.1 EFT and Wess-Zumino terms The EW anomalies are cured in the UV with the presence of anomalon fields, which are chiral undertheU(1) andtransformnon-triviallyundertheEWgroup. In this setup X decays predominantly into neutrinos, for instance in models based on a П„-flavor sym- metry whenever m < 2m , so that the leading probes are rare processes with invisible final X П„ states. Forgenericchargeassignments, anomalycancellationrequiresnewfermionsthatarechi- ralunderU(1) andcarryEWquantumnumbers. Whentheirmassespredominantlyarisefrom X U(1) breaking, integrating them out yields an infrared EFT supplemented by WessвҖ“Zumino X operators whose coefficients are fixed by mixed-anomaly matching. Within this EFT, WZ operators induce Z вҶ’ ОіX at tree level, while the XWвҲӮW operator generates loop-induced flavor-changing couplings following a predictive minimal-flavor-violation pattern. We have surveyed the resulting missing-energy probes in radiative Z decays and in rare K, D, and B transitions, and summarized the corresponding bounds in the (m ,g ) plane X X for representative benchmarks. In particular, we emphasized the complementarity between Z вҶ’ ОіE and rare-meson searches, and illustrated the framework in the gauged П„-flavor miss benchmark relevant to Belle II and NA62/KOTO.

On the UV side, we classified minimal electroweak-anomalon spectra solving the mixed- anomaly conditions for N вүӨ 4, and combined phenomenological requirements with finite- ОЁ naturalness considerations to constrain the anomalon mass scale.
